full_response:
Let n = |V|.  
Because T is a tree it has exactly n–1 edges, and for every ordered source vertex s there is a unique simple path in T from s to any other vertex x.  
If we can traverse that path while maintaining the largest‐weight edge seen so far, we obtain max[s,x].  
Repeating this traversal once for every source vertex touches Θ(n) edges each time, hence Θ(n²) edges in total – optimal, because the output itself has Θ(n²) entries.

-------------------------------------------------
Algorithm
-------------------------------------------------
We number the vertices 1 … n and store T in adjacency lists.

proc ALL-PAIRS-MAX(T)
    Input : adjacency list of tree T with edge weights w(·)
    Output: n × n table M such that M[u][v] = max edge weight on
            the unique path between u and v in T
-----------------------------------------------------------------
1  let M be an n × n array initialised to –∞
2  for each vertex s ∈ {1,…,n} do              ▹ n different sources
3        mark[1…n] ← false
4        DFS(s , s , –∞)                       ▹ start depth-first search
5  return M

proc DFS(source, v, currentMax)
    mark[v] ← true
    M[source][v] ← currentMax          ▹ when v = source, store –∞
    for each edge (v, u) of weight w(v,u) with mark[u] = false do
          DFS(source , u , max(currentMax , w(v,u)))
-----------------------------------------------------------------

(Optionally set M[u][u] = –∞ or leave the diagonal undefined.)

-------------------------------------------------
Correctness proof
-------------------------------------------------
We prove by induction on the recursion depth of DFS that when the call  
DFS(s , v , m) is executed, the parameter m equals

      max { w(e) | e lies on the unique path P(s,v) in T }.

Base step (depth 0): v = s and m = –∞, which is the maximum of an empty set, so the property holds.

Inductive step: Assume the property holds for the call DFS(s , v , m).
When the algorithm explores an unvisited neighbour u via edge e = (v,u)
it issues DFS(s , u , m’ ) with m’ = max(m , w(e)).
The unique path P(s,u) is P(s,v) followed by e, so the largest edge on
P(s,u) is either the largest edge on P(s,v) (value m) or w(e).
Thus m’ is exactly the required maximum, and the induction goes through.

Consequently, when DFS finishes, M[s][v] stores max[s,v] for every v.
Since the outer loop calls DFS once for every source s, the table M
contains the correct value for every ordered pair (s,v).  
Symmetry of the tree path implies M[u][v] = M[v][u], so the algorithm
indeed computes the required quantity for all unordered pairs as well.

-------------------------------------------------
Complexity analysis
-------------------------------------------------
Each DFS visits every edge of the tree once, taking Θ(n) time.
The outer loop performs n such DFS traversals, so the total running time is Θ(n²).  
The table M stores n² entries, hence Θ(n²) space, which is optimal for explicitly outputting all max[u,v] values.

\boxed{\text{Run a DFS (or BFS) from every vertex; while traversing, keep the
maximum edge weight seen so far.  This fills an n\times n table in }\Theta(n^{2})\text{ time and space, hence
computes } \text{max}[u,v]\ \forall u,v\in V.}

Confidence: 0.34
